policy_statement: Develop and promote sharing table and donation guidance for P-12 schools through a DEC partnership with the State Education Department and DOH.
mechanism_description: DEC plans to partner with state agencies to develop and promote sharing-table and donation guidance in schools to increase redistribution of edible food, reducing food waste disposal and avoiding landfill methane.
primary_causal_pathway: Food system waste handling change → ↓ organic waste landfilled → ↓ anaerobic decomposition in landfill → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ GHG forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Schools and institutions implement sharing tables, food recovery, and donation logistics so edible surplus is captured and redistributed instead of discarded.  
2) Mediating physical process: Less food enters landfills, reducing anaerobic breakdown of organics that generates methane; potentially also reduces landfill gas generation rates.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane emissions from landfills (and associated GHG reductions), contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: Increasing edible food donation/redistribution primarily reduces food sent to landfill, avoiding anaerobic decomposition and thus reducing methane (GHG) emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Redistribution reduces food waste disposal → less landfill methane → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “resource efficiency” (better use of edible food) is present, the stated and most climate-salient physical mechanism is explicit landfill methane avoidance, which is direct mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some food donation programs may yield larger upstream production-avoidance benefits (reduced agricultural emissions) or may shift waste to composting/AD rather than donation; however, this canonical mechanism is specifically “food waste reduction and landfill methane avoidance,” keeping mitigation dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy centered on donation/redistribution to reduce disposed food consistently reduces methane-generating landfill inputs, making mitigation the primary category across implementations.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary tags (e.g., resource efficiency, social co-benefits, upstream supply-chain effects) require policy-specific evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If donated food displaces other food purchases, upstream production emissions reductions could be material, but this varies widely and is harder to guarantee than landfill methane avoidance. If the baseline disposal pathway is not landfill (e.g., widespread composting/AD), the mitigation magnitude changes, though the intended mechanism still targets methane avoidance from landfilling.
key_indicators: “develop and promote”; “sharing table and donation guidance”; “partner with the State Education Department”; “New York State Department of Health (DOH)”; “P-12 schools”
co_benefits: Reduced food insecurity (increased edible food donation); reduced solid waste disposal costs; student/community health and nutrition benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a “planning-strategy” due to the interagency coordination, but the core instrument described is guidance (information/toolkit) rather than a plan with targets or funded implementation.
